preamble: The shift from the postalveolar fricative [ʃ] to a glottal stop [ʔ] is a severe form of debuccalization. It represents the final stage of lenition where the sibilant loses its oral place of articulation (the tongue-to-palate friction) and is reduced to a simple glottal gesture, typically in syllable-coda positions.

examples.note: In dialects where final sibilants are heavily aspirated, the sound can further reduce to a weak glottal stop or trigger gemination of the following consonant.
Widespread reduction of word-final voiceless consonants to glottal stops.
